Problem:
Alice the architect and Bob the builder play a game. First, Alice
 chooses two points $P$ and $Q$ in the plane and a subset $S$ of
 the plane, which are announced to Bob. Next, Bob marks infinitely
 many points in the plane, designating each a city. He may not place
 two cities within distance at most one unit of each other, and no
 three cities he places may be collinear. Finally, roads are constructed
 between the cities as follows: for each pair $A,B$ of cities, they
 are connected with a road along the line segment $AB$ if and only
 if the following condition holds: For every city $C$ distinct from
 $A$ and $B$, there exists $R\in S$ such that $\triangle PQR$ is
 directly similar to either $\triangle ABC$ or $\triangle BAC$. Alice
 wins the game if

 \noindent (i) the resulting roads allow for travel between any pair
 of cities via a finite sequence of roads and

 \noindent (ii) no two roads cross.

 \noindent Otherwise, Bob wins. Determine, with proof, which player
 has a winning strategy. (Note: $\triangle UVW$ is directly similar
 to $\triangle XYZ$ if there exists a sequence of rotations, translations,
 and dilations sending $U$ to $X$, $V$ to $Y$, and $W$ to $Z$.)
Solution:
The answer is that Alice wins. Let's define a Bob-set $V$ to be
 a set of points in the plane with no three collinear and with all
 distances at least 1 . The point of the problem is to prove the following
 fact.

 Claim - Given a Bob-set $V\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{2}$, consider the
 Bob-graph with vertex set $V$ defined as follows: draw edge $ab$
 if and only if the disk with diameter $\overline{ab}$ contains no
 other points of $V$ on or inside it. Then the Bob-graph is connected
 and planar.

 Proving this claim shows that Alice wins since Alice can specify $\mathcal{S}$
 to be the set of points outside the disk of diameter $PQ$.

 We start with proving that every Bob-graph is connected. Assume for
 contradiction the graph is disconnected. Let $p$ and $q$ be two
 points in different connected components. Since $pq$ is not an edge,
 there exists a third point $r$ inside the disk with diameter $\overline{pq}$.

 Hence, $r$ is in a different connected component from at least one
 of $p$ or $q$ - let's say point $p$. Then we repeat the same argument
 on the disk with diameter $\overline{pr}$ to find a new point $s$,
 non-adjacent to either $p$ or $r$. See the figure below, where the
 X'ed out dashed edges indicate points which are not only non-adjacent
 but in different connected components.

 In this way we generate an infinite sequence of distances $\delta_{1},\delta_{2},\delta_{3},\ldots$
 among the non-edges in the picture above. By the "Pythagorean theorem"
 (or really the inequality for it), we have

 \[
 \delta_{i}^{2}\leq\delta_{i-1}^{2}-1
 \]

 and this eventually generates a contradiction for large $i$, since
 we get $0\leq\delta_{i}^{2}\leq\delta_{1}^{2}-(i-$ $1)$.

 Now we prove that every Bob-graph is planar. Assume for contradiction
 that edges $ac$ and $bd$ meet, meaning $abcd$ is a convex quadrilateral.
 WLOG assume $\angle bad\geq90^{\circ}$ (each quadrilateral has an
 angle at least $90^{\circ}$ ). Then the disk with diameter $\overline{bd}$
 contains $a$, contradiction.

 2. Constructed $S$ as above and proved that the resulting graph is planar.
(Almost)
 1. Constructed $S$ as above and proved that the resulting graph is connected.

Proof.  
Let Alice fix two distinct points P,Q in the plane and a subset S⊆ℝ².  We show that Bob can then place infinitely many “cities,” satisfying “no two within distance ≤1” and “no three collinear,” so that either the road‐graph is disconnected or it is nonplanar.  In either event Alice’s requirements (i) connectivity and (ii) planarity both fail, so Bob wins.  

1.  Shape‐parameterization.  
Define for any noncollinear ordered triple U,V,W the “shape” 
   shape(ΔUVW) := (∠UWV, ∠VWU),
where both angles lie in (0,π) and sum to <π.  Thus all possible shapes form the open triangle 
   T = {(α,β): α>0, β>0, α+β<π}  ⊂ℝ².  

Alice’s choice of S induces the subset 
   D_S := {shape(ΔPQR): R∈S and P,Q,R are noncollinear}  ⊆T.
If some R∈S is collinear with P,Q it simply does not contribute a shape, which causes no difficulty.

2.  Road‐criterion in terms of shapes.  
By the game‐rules, two distinct cities A,B are joined by a road precisely when for every third city C there exists R∈S such that ΔPQR is directly similar to either ΔABC or ΔBAC.  Direct similarity preserves the pair of base‐angles at A,B (in order), so this is equivalent to the single condition 
   for each third city C,
     shape(ΔABC) ∈ D_S  or  shape(ΔBAC) ∈ D_S.
Since shape(ΔBAC) just reorders the two base‐angles, it also lies in T.  

Hence equivalently:
   A and B are joined by a road ⇔ for every third city C, shape(ΔABC)∈D_S.  

3.  Bob’s dichotomous placement.  
We now describe one uniform way for Bob to place his cities (infinitely many) on a circle, and then split into two exhaustive cases of D_S.

–  Bob chooses a small central‐angle δ>0 and places countably infinitely many points equally spaced by δ on a circle of radius r.  Then no three are collinear, and by choosing r sufficiently large one ensures each chord 2r·sin(δ/2)>1, so no two cities lie within distance ≤1.  Moreover, if δ is small enough, every inscribed triangle among these cities has its pair of base angles at A,B very small—hence all such shapes lie in some prescribed small open ball U⊂T.

Case 1:  D_S≠T.  
Since T is open and convex, T∖D_S is a nonempty open region.  Pick an open ball U⊂T∖D_S.  Then by making δ small we guarantee that for every triple of Bob’s cities A,B,C the shape(ΔABC)∈U.  But U∩D_S=∅, so for each pair (A,B) and for the particular third city C, shape(ΔABC)∉D_S.  Hence the road‐criterion fails already for that C, and no road is built between A and B.  In fact no two cities ever receive a road, so the road‐graph has no edges and is totally disconnected.  Alice’s connectivity requirement (i) fails.  

Case 2:  D_S=T.  
Now every possible shape in T occurs as shape(ΔPQR) for some R∈S.  Place Bob’s cities equally spaced on a (large) circle so that they are in strictly convex position (again no three collinear, chords >1).  Then for any two cities A,B and any third city C the shape(ΔABC)∈T=D_S.  By definition of D_S there is R∈S with shape(ΔPQR)=shape(ΔABC), hence ΔABC∼ΔPQR or ΔBAC∼ΔPQR and so A,B become joined by a road.  Since C was arbitrary, every pair (A,B) is connected directly.  The resulting road‐graph is the complete graph on infinitely many points in convex position, which necessarily has crossing edges (it contains arbitrarily large non‐planar complete subgraphs).  Alice’s planarity requirement (ii) fails.  

4.  Conclusion.  
In either case Bob forces one of Alice’s two winning conditions to fail.  Therefore Bob has a winning strategy for every choice of P,Q,S, and Alice cannot win.  ■

Problem:
Let $H$ be the orthocenter of acute triangle $ABC$, let $F$ be
 the foot of the altitude from $C$ to $AB$, and let $P$ be the reflection
 of $H$ across $BC$. Suppose that the circumcircle of triangle $AFP$
 intersects line $BC$ at two distinct points $X$ and $Y$. Prove
 that $C$ is the midpoint of $XY$.
Solution:
Let $Q$ be the antipode of $B$ in the circumcircle of $ABC$.


 \textbf{Claim.} $AHQC$ is a parallelogram, and $APCQ$ is an isosceles
 trapezoid.



  \emph{Proof.} The claim follows from $\overline{AH}\perp\overline{BC}\perp\overline{CQ}$
 and $\overline{CF}\perp\overline{AB}\perp\overline{AQ}$.


 Let $M$ be the midpoint of $\overline{QC}$.


 \textbf{Claim.} Point $M$ is the circumcenter of $\triangle AFP$.


 \emph{Proof.} It's clear that $MA=MP$ from the isosceles trapezoid
 $APCQ$. As for $MA=MF$, let $N$ denote the midpoint of $\overline{AF}$;
 then $\overline{MN}$ is a midline of the parallelogram, so $\overline{MN}\perp\overline{AF}$.


 Since $\overline{CM}\perp\overline{BC}$ and $M$ is the center of
 $(AFP)$, it follows $CX=CY$.

(Almost)
  1. Solution is almost complete, but made minor mistakes which are not negligible.

Problem:
Determine, with proof, all positive integers $k$ such that
 \[
 \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=0}^{n}\binom{n}{i}^{k}
 \]
 is an integer for every positive integer $n$.
Solution:
The answer is all even $k$. Let's abbreviate $S(n):=\binom{n}{0}^{k}+\cdots+\binom{n}{n}^{k}$
 for the sum in the problem.


 \textbf{Proof that even $k$ is necessary.}

  Choose $n=2$. We need $3\mid S(2)=2+2^{k}$, which requires $k$
 to be even.


 \textbf{Proof that even $k$ is sufficient. }

 From now on we treat $k$ as fixed, and we let $p^{e}$ be a prime
 fully dividing $n+1$. It is sufficient to prove that $S(n)$ is a
 multiple of $p^{e}$ for each such $p^{e}$. The basic idea is to
 reduce from $n+1$ to $(n+1)/p$ by an induction. We prove the following
 lemma on each individual binomial coefficient.


 \textbf{Lemma. }Suppose $p^{e}$ is a prime power which fully divides
 $n+1$. Then

 \[
 \binom{n}{i}\equiv\pm\binom{\frac{n+1}{p}-1}{\lfloor i/p\rfloor}\quad\left(\bmod p^{e}\right)
 \]

 \emph{Proof.} If we write
 \[
 \binom{n}{i}=\prod_{1\leq j\leq i}\frac{n-(j-1)}{j}
 \]

 then the fractions for $p\nmid j$ are all $\pm1\left(\bmod p^{e}\right)$.
 So only considers those $j$ with $p\mid j$; in that case one obtains
 the claimed $\binom{\frac{n+1}{p}-1}{\lfloor i/p\rfloor}$ exactly
 (even without having to take modulo $p^{e}$).


 From the lemma, it follows if $p^{e}$ is a prime power which fully
 divides $n+1$, then
 \[
 S(n)\equiv p\cdot S\left(\frac{n+1}{p}-1\right)\quad\left(\bmod p^{e}\right)
 \]

 by grouping the $n+1$ terms (for $0\leq i\leq n$ ) into consecutive
 ranges of length $p$ (by the value of $\lfloor i/p\rfloor$ ) and
 the result follows by induction.
Grading guidelines:
(Partial)
 1. Proved that $k$ should be even.
 2. Used the induction on $n+1$ and considered the equation for$\frac{n+1}{p}$.
 3. Stated the identity $n+1\mid\sum_{i=0}^{n}\left((-1)^{i}\binom{n}{i}\right)^{k}$ without proof.
(Almost)
 1. Solution is almost complete, but made minor mistakes which are not negligible, such as coming up with a proof that only works for odd primes.
Response:
The claim is that the expression

      A_{n,k}  =  \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{i=0}^n \binom{n}{i}^k

is an integer for every positive integer n if and only if k is even.

Proof.

1.  No odd k works.  Suppose k is odd.  Take n=2.  Then

      \sum_{i=0}^2 \binom{2}{i}^k  =  \binom{2}{0}^k + \binom{2}{1}^k + \binom{2}{2}^k
                               =  1 + 2^k + 1
                               =  2^k + 2.

  Since k is odd, write k=2m+1.  Then 2^k = 2^{2m+1} = 2·(2^{2m}), and one checks

      2^{2m} ≡ 1  (mod 3),
  so
      2^k ≡ 2·1 = 2  (mod 3),
  hence
      2^k + 2 ≡ 2 + 2 = 4 ≡ 1  (mod 3).

  In particular 3 does not divide 2^k+2, so

      A_{2,k} = (2^k + 2)/3

  is not an integer.  Thus no odd k can make A_{n,k} integral for all n.

2.  Every even k works.  Now suppose k is even, say k=2m.  We must show that for each positive n,

      S = \sum_{i=0}^n \binom{n}{i}^{2m}

  is divisible by n+1.  Equivalently, we prove that every prime divisor p of n+1 divides S.

  Let p be any prime with p | (n+1).  Then n ≡ −1 (mod p).  For each i = 0,1,…,n we write the binomial coefficient in product form:

      \binom{n}{i}
      = \frac{n(n−1)···(n−i+1)}{i!}
      ≡ \frac{(−1)(−2)···(−i)}{i!}  (mod p)
      = \frac{(−1)^i · i!}{i!}  (mod p)
      = (−1)^i   (mod p).

  Since k = 2m is even,

      \binom{n}{i}^k  ≡  ((−1)^i)^{2m}  =  (−1)^{2mi}  = 1  (mod p).

  Therefore each term of the sum S is congruent to 1 mod p, and there are (n+1) terms.  Hence

      S ≡ 1 + 1 + ··· + 1  (n+1 times)  =  n+1  (mod p)
      ≡ 0  (mod p).

  Since p was an arbitrary prime divisor of n+1, it follows that n+1 divides S.  Thus

      A_{n,k}  =  S/(n+1)

  is an integer for every n.

Combining steps 1 and 2, we conclude that exactly the even positive integers k make

      \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{i=0}^n \binom{n}{i}^k

an integer for all positive n.  This completes the proof. ∎

Problem:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers with $m\ge n$. There are
 $m$ cupcakes of different flavors arranged around a circle and $n$
 people who like cupcakes. Each person assigns a non-negative real
 number score to each cupcake, depending on how much they like the
 cupcake. Suppose that for each person $P$, it is possible to partition
 the circle of $m$ cupcakes into $n$ groups of consecutive cupcakes
 so that the sum of $P$'s scores of the cupcakes in each group is
 at least $1$. Prove that it is possible to distribute the $m$ cupcakes
 to the $n$ people so that each person $P$ receives cupcakes of total
 score at least $1$ with respect to $P$.
Solution:
Arbitrarily pick any one person - call her Pip - and her $n$ arcs.
 The initial idea is to try to apply Hall's marriage lemma to match
 the $n$ people with Pip's arcs (such that each such person is happy
 with their matched arc). To that end, construct the obvious bipartite
 graph $\mathfrak{G}$ between the people and the arcs for Pip.

 We now consider the following algorithm, which takes several steps.
 \begin{itemize}
 \item If a perfect matching of $\mathfrak{G}$ exists, we're done!
 \item We're probably not that lucky. Per Hall's condition, this means there
 is a bad set $\mathcal{B}_{1}$ of people, who are compatible with
 fewer than $\left|\mathcal{B}_{1}\right|$ of the arcs. Then delete
 $\mathcal{B}_{1}$ and the neighbors of $\mathcal{B}_{1}$, then try
 to find a matching on the remaining graph.
 \item If a matching exists now, terminate the algorithm. Otherwise, that
 means there's another bad set $\mathcal{B}_{2}$ for the remaining
 graph. We again delete $\mathcal{B}_{2}$ and the fewer than $\mathcal{B}_{2}$
 neighbors.
 \item Repeat until some perfect matching $\mathcal{M}$ is possible in the
 remaining graph, i.e. there are no more bad sets (and then terminate
 once that occurs).
 \end{itemize}
 Since Pip is a universal vertex, it's impossible to delete Pip, so
 the algorithm does indeed terminate with nonempty $\mathcal{M}$.

 We commit to assigning each of person in $\mathcal{M}$ their matched
 arc (in particular if there are no bad sets at all, the problem is
 already solved). Now we finish the problem by induction on $n$ (for
 the remaining people) by simply deleting the arcs used up by $\mathcal{M}$.

 To see why this deletion-induction works, consider any particular
 person Quinn not in $\mathcal{M}$. By definition, Quinn is not happy
 with any of the $\operatorname{arcs}$ in $\mathcal{M}$. So when
 an $\operatorname{arc}\mathcal{A}$ of $\mathcal{M}$ is deleted,
 it had value less than 1 for Quinn so in particular it couldn't contain
 entirely any of Quinn's arcs. Hence at most one endpoint among Quinn's
 arcs was in the deleted $\operatorname{arc}\mathcal{A}$. When this
 happens, this causes two arcs of Quinn to merge, and the merged value
 is

 \[
 (\geq1)+(\geq1)-(\leq1)\quad\geq1
 \]

 meaning the induction is OK.
Grading guidelines:
(Partial)
 1. Observed that when an arc with scores smaller than $1$ to some person $X$ is deleted, the problem condition still holds for $X$.
 2. Applied Hall's theorem to the arcs of a fixed person and the $n$ people.
(Almost)
 1. Found a perfect matching and uses induction, but didn't explain why the induction works.
 2. Full proof, but didn't mention that there exists a nonempty set that satisfies Hall's condition.

{"reasoning":"The elementary inductive proof (Attempt 3) is the most transparent and avoids heavy topology. Its key insight is: pick one person’s (say P₁’s) partition into n contiguous arcs of score ≥1; for each other person Pᵢ, at most one of those arcs can “block” Pᵢ (i.e.\ make it impossible to partition the complement into n−1 high‐score pieces); hence at most n−1 arcs are blocked overall, so at least one remains. Give that arc to P₁ and apply induction to the other n−1 people on the leftover circle. The only nontrivial part is rigorously proving that one person Pᵢ cannot block two different arcs—this follows from a simple combinatorial argument on the circle. We combine this clear, combinatorial reasoning into a single self‐contained proof.","response":"Complete Proof:

We restate the problem for clarity.  Let m,n be positive integers with m≥n.  Around a circle are m distinct cupcakes in fixed clockwise order.  There are n people P₁,…,Pₙ.  Each person P assigns a nonnegative real score s_P(j) to cupcake j.  We assume:

(*) For every person P, there exists a way to cut the circle into n contiguous (cyclic) groups (possibly of different sizes), each having total P‐score at least 1.

We must show:  One can distribute the m cupcakes into n disjoint contiguous groups, assigning one group to each person, so that each person P receives a group whose total P‐score is at least 1.

Proof by induction on n.

Base case n=1:
There is only one person P₁.  Hypothesis (*) says P₁ can cut the circle into 1 contiguous group of score ≥1; that group is the whole circle, so ∑_{j=1}^m s_{P₁}(j) ≥1.  Give all cupcakes to P₁, and the requirement is met.

Inductive step:
Assume the statement holds for n−1 (with any m′≥n−1).  Now consider n people P₁,…,Pₙ and m≥n cupcakes satisfying (*).

1.  By (*), person P₁ has some partition of the circle into n contiguous arcs A₁,A₂,…,Aₙ (in clockwise order) each of which has P₁‐score at least 1:
     ∑_{j∈A_k} s_{P₁}(j) ≥1  for k=1,…,n.

2.  We will choose one of these arcs A_k to give to P₁, then use induction to distribute the remaining cupcakes among the other n−1 people.  Some arcs A_k might be “blocked” for a person Pᵢ (i≥2) in that removing A_k leaves a remainder that Pᵢ cannot split into n−1 contiguous high‐score pieces.

  Definition (blocked by Pᵢ):  For i=2,…,n and k∈{1,…,n}, say “k is blocked by Pᵢ” if, when we remove A_k (i.e.\ delete its cupcakes), the complementary set of cupcakes (forming one or two arcs on the circle) does not admit any partition into n−1 contiguous arcs each of Pᵢ‐score ≥1.

3.  Key Claim:  Each person Pᵢ (i=2,…,n) can block at most one index k.

  Proof of Claim:
  Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, that Pᵢ blocks two distinct arcs, say A_k and A_ℓ with k≠ℓ.  Then by definition:
   • Removing A_k prevents any partition of the remainder into n−1 high‐score arcs for Pᵢ; equivalently, in every partition of the full circle into n contiguous arcs by Pᵢ, each of those n arcs must intersect A_k.
   • Similarly, each of Pᵢ’s n arcs must also intersect A_ℓ.

  Let B₁,B₂,…,Bₙ be any partition of the full circle into n contiguous arcs each of Pᵢ‐score ≥1 (which exists by hypothesis (*) for Pᵢ).  Then by the above, each B_j meets A_k and also meets A_ℓ.

  However, A_k and A_ℓ are two disjoint contiguous arcs on the circle (they come from P₁’s partition).  The union A_k ∪ A_ℓ therefore splits the circle into exactly two connected ‘‘gaps.’’  Because the B_j’s are themselves disjoint arcs whose union is the whole circle, at least one of these two gaps must lie entirely inside some B_j (otherwise the B_j’s could not cover the circle without overlap).  But any B_j contained in a gap does not intersect either A_k or A_ℓ, contradicting the requirement that every B_j hits both A_k and A_ℓ.  Hence Pᵢ cannot block two different k’s.  This proves the Claim.

4.  Since there are n−1 other people P₂,…,Pₙ and each blocks at most one of the n arcs, at most n−1 arcs are blocked in total.  Therefore at least one index k₀∈{1,…,n} is not blocked by any Pᵢ.

5.  Give the arc A_{k₀} to P₁.  By choice of k₀, for each i=2,…,n, removing A_{k₀} leaves the remaining cupcakes splittable by Pᵢ into n−1 contiguous arcs each of Pᵢ‐score ≥1.

6.  Now we have n−1 people (P₂,…,Pₙ) and m−|A_{k₀}| ≥ n−1 cupcakes arranged in one or two cyclic arcs.  Apply the induction hypothesis to these n−1 people on that leftover arrangement: they can be assigned the remaining cupcakes in n−1 contiguous groups so that each Pᵢ (i≥2) gets score ≥1.

7.  Putting it all together, we have distributed all m cupcakes: P₁ got A_{k₀}, and each other person Pᵢ got one of the n−1 arcs from the inductive step.  By construction, each Pᵢ’s received block has total score ≥1.

This completes the inductive proof.  Therefore for every m≥n satisfying (*), one can distribute the cupcakes so each person receives total score at least 1.  ■
